1: 'Vindicate me, O God, and plead my cause against an ungodly nation; from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me' — the legal petition that opens Ps 43 (the second half of the combined Ps 42-43 psalm). 'Vindicate me' (shaphteni, judge me, render a verdict for me) — the appeal for divine adjudication in a legal dispute. The psalmist is surrounded by an 'ungodly nation' (goy lo-chasid, a people without covenant loyalty) — suggesting the exile or dispersion context where the psalmist is surrounded by non-Israelites whose court systems do not recognize YHWH's authority. The appeal bypasses human courts to the divine judge.
2: 'For you are the God who is my stronghold; why have you rejected me? Why do I go about mourning under the oppression of the enemy?' — the same paradox as 42:9: YHWH named as 'God of my stronghold' (el ma'uzi, the God of my refuge/fortress) while experiencing what feels like divine rejection (zemachtani, you have cast me off). The paradox is not resolved by denying either side: the psalmist holds both the covenant identity of YHWH (fortress) and the experiential reality (rejection) simultaneously, pressing both into the petition. The mourning walk under enemy oppression is the physical enactment of covenant grief.
3: 'Send out your light and your truth; let them lead me, let them bring me to your holy mountain and to your dwelling' — light (orcha) and truth/faithfulness (amitecha) are personified as divine messengers sent to escort the psalmist back to the temple. The 'holy mountain' (har qodshecha) is Zion; the 'dwelling' (mishkanotecha, your tents/tabernacles) is the sanctuary complex. The request to be led back to the temple is both geographical (physical return from exile or exile-like distance) and spiritual: to be brought back into the place of covenant encounter. Light and truth are the qualities of divine guidance that make the path findable.
4: 'Then I will approach the altar of God, to God who is my greatest joy, and I will praise you with the lyre, O God, my God' — the todah (thanksgiving) vow: if YHWH brings the psalmist back to the sanctuary, worship and praise will follow. 'My greatest joy' (simchat gili, the joy of my rejoicing) — the temple and its worship as the psalmist's supreme source of delight, contrasted with all other pleasures. The lyre (kinor) was the instrument of the temple singers (1 Chr 15:16; 25:1-6; Ps 33:2; 71:22). The anticipated praise is not merely emotional but liturgical and performative.
5: 'Why are you downcast, O my soul? Why are you in turmoil within me? Put your hope in God; for I will yet praise him, my Savior and my God' — the third and final occurrence of the combined psalm's refrain. The refrain here comes not after additional lament but as the conclusion of the petition (Ps 43:1-4): having cried out, having asked for divine guidance, having vowed the return to the altar, the soul now addresses itself with the settled conviction of the first two refrains. The three-fold repetition creates a rhetorical and liturgical completion: the petition has been made, the vow declared, and the soul's answer to its own despair is the covenant affirmation.
